File content (41528 tokens) exceeds maximum allowed tokens (25000). Use offset and limit parameters to read specific portions of the file, or search for specific content instead of reading the whole file.

5236	\section{Sheaf internal-hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (the \(\otimes\)-inverse
5237	  infrastructure)}
5238	\label{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}
5239	
5240	The PRIMARY group law (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) needs a
5241	\(\otimes\)-inverse for every line bundle, supplied by
5242	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} as the dual
5243	\(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\). The standard route
5244	constructs that dual as a special case of the \emph{sheaf internal hom} of
5245	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; over a fixed ring the internal hom of \(R\)-modules is the
5246	linear-coyoneda module \(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,N = (M \to N)\), with dual
5247	\(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,\mathbf{1} = M^\vee\), but for the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
5248	no such
5249	internal-hom / monoidal-closed structure exists at the
5250	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\), \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\), or general-categorical
5251	level. This section decomposes the construction of the missing object into
5252	individually-formalizable sub-steps, following the slice presentation of the internal
5253	hom; each block names the intended Lean declaration and its dependencies. The
5254	specialisation that closes \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is recorded in
5255	\cref{rem:dual_discharges_inverse}; an alternative descent-theoretic route is noted in
5256	\cref{rem:dual_via_stack}.
5257	
5258	\emph{Status: deferred, off the group's critical path.} The blocks of this section
5259	--- the dual object \(\mathtt{dual}\) and its restriction compatibility
5260	\cref{lem:dual_restrict_iso}, the local-triviality of the dual
5261	\cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}, the gluing engine
5262	\cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}, and the discharge remark
5263	\cref{rem:dual_discharges_inverse} --- together constitute the \emph{deferred}
5264	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftrightarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) bridge: on a
5265	scheme an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is locally free of rank one, and
5266	conversely (Stacks tag 0B8M), so the two carriers agree, and the locally-trivial
5267	carrier's missing inverse (\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}) is recovered
5268	precisely from the dual built here.
5269	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 0B8M (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
5270	% modules), Lemma lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1
5271	% (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4159-L4165).
5272	% SOURCE QUOTE:
5273	% "\begin{lemma}
5274	% \label{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}
5275	% Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
5276	% $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
5277	% If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
5278	% the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case).
5279	% \end{lemma}"
5280	However, the relative Picard \emph{group law} is now carried on the
5281	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
5282	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}, \cref{thm:pic_commgroup}), where the inverse is
5283	the membership witness and \emph{no} dual object is constructed. This section is
5284	therefore needed only by a downstream consumer that specifically requires the
5285	locally-trivial form of an inverse --- not by the group law. The blocks are retained
5286	intact for that future use.
5287	
5288	A structural point governs the whole construction. The naive rule
5289	\(U \mapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) is \emph{contravariant} in the
5290	restriction maps of \(M\) (a restriction \(M(U) \to M(V)\) for \(V \subseteq U\)
5291	induces precomposition \(\Hom(M(V), N(V)) \to \Hom(M(U), \cdot)\) in the wrong
5292	direction), so it is \emph{not} a presheaf in the covariant sense and cannot be
5293	sheafified like the tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The remedy is
5294	the \emph{slice} formula \(U \mapsto (M|_U \to N|_U)\) --- the module of morphisms of
5295	\emph{restricted} modules over the restricted ring --- whose presheaf functoriality
5296	(restrict a global-over-\(U\) morphism to a global-over-\(V\) morphism, for
5297	\(V \subseteq U\)) \emph{is} covariant. Constructing that slice presheaf, and
5298	proving it satisfies the sheaf condition, is the substance of the build.
5299	
5300	\begin{definition}
5301	
5302	\leanok
5303	[The global-ring module on morphisms of presheaves of modules (value module)]
5304	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
5305	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.homModule}
5306	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
5307	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
5308	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
5309	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3508--L3514).
5310	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
5311	  % "abelian groups. In addition, given an element
5312	  % $\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
5313	  % and a section $f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ then we can define
5314	  % $f\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
5315	  % by either precomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{F}|_U$
5316	  % or postcomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{G}|_U$ (it gives
5317	  % the same result). Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
5318	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
5319	  (tag area 01CM): the section ring acts on a morphism of restricted modules by
5320	  post-composition with multiplication by the section.}
5321	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a base category \(\mathcal{C}\)
5322	  that has a terminal object \(T\), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\).
5323	  The abelian group of morphisms \(M \longrightarrow N\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules
5324	  carries a canonical \(R(T)\)-module structure --- the \emph{morphism module}
5325	  \(\mathtt{homModule}\,M\,N\). A global scalar \(f \in R(T)\) acts on a morphism
5326	  \(\varphi : M \to N\) by post-composition with multiplication by \(f\),
5327	  \[
5328	    f \cdot \varphi \;:=\; m_f^{N} \circ \varphi,
5329	  \]
5330	  where \(m_f^{N} : N \to N\) is the \emph{multiplication-by-\(f\)} endomorphism of
5331	  \(N\): the endomorphism that, on each object \(U \in \mathcal{C}\), is multiplication
5332	  by the restriction \(f|_U \in R(U)\) of \(f\) along the unique morphism \(U \to T\)
5333	  to the terminal object. The module axioms hold because the assignment
5334	  \(f \mapsto m_f^{N}\) is a ring homomorphism \(R(T) \to \mathrm{End}(N)\) into the
5335	  endomorphism ring of \(N\) --- a product \(f \cdot g\) goes to the \emph{composite}
5336	  \(m_f^{N} \circ m_g^{N}\) of the two multiplication endomorphisms, the order in which
5337	  the factors compose being reversed by the fact that the scalar acts by
5338	  \emph{post}-composition --- and composition of morphisms is biadditive in the
5339	  preadditive category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). Distributivity and the
5340	  associativity of the action then follow from bilinearity of composition together with
5341	  the homomorphism property of \(f \mapsto m_f^{N}\). This is the genuinely new core of
5342	  the construction: Mathlib supplies the \emph{fixed}-ring internal hom
5343	  \(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,N = (M \to N)\) over a single base ring, but nothing equipping the
5344	  morphism group with the module structure for the \emph{varying} structure presheaf at
5345	  the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
5346	\end{definition}
5347	
5348	\begin{definition}
5349	
5350	\leanok
5351	[Slice specialisation of the morphism module]
5352	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}
5353	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.internalHomObjModule}
5354	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
5355	  Specialise \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} to a slice category. For an object
5356	  (open) \(U\) of \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\), restricting along the forgetful functor
5357	  \(\mathtt{Over.forget}\,U : \mathcal{C}/U \to \mathcal{C}\) --- the open-immersion
5358	  restriction \(M \mapsto M|_U\) --- carries \(M, N\) to presheaves of modules
5359	  \(M|_U, N|_U\) over the restricted structure ring on the slice \(\mathcal{C}/U\). The
5360	  slice \(\mathcal{C}/U\) has the terminal object \(\mathtt{Over.mk}\,(\mathrm{id}_U)\),
5361	  at which the restricted ring takes the value \(R(U)\); hence
5362	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} makes the morphism group
5363	  \(M|_U \longrightarrow N|_U\) an \(R(U)\)-module, the
5364	  \(\mathtt{internalHomObjModule}\). This \(R(U)\)-module \((M|_U \to N|_U)\) is exactly
5365	  the value \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}.
5366	\end{definition}
5367	
5368	\begin{definition}
5369	
5370	\leanok
5371	[Presheaf internal hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
5372	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
5373	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.internalHom}
5374	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:presheaf_internal_hom_value,
5375	        def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value, lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
5376	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
5377	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
5378	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3500--L3524).
5379	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
5380	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
5381	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
5382	  % Consider the rule
5383	  % $$
5384	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
5385	  % $$
5386	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
5387	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
5388	  % abelian groups. In addition, given an element
5389	  % $\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
5390	  % and a section $f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ then we can define
5391	  % $f\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
5392	  % by either precomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{F}|_U$
5393	  % or postcomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{G}|_U$ (it gives
5394	  % the same result). Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
5395	  % We will denote this sheaf
5396	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})$.
5397	  % There is a canonical ``evaluation'' morphism
5398	  % $$
5399	  % \mathcal{F}
5400	  % \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
5401	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
5402	  % \longrightarrow
5403	  % \mathcal{G}.
5404	  % $$"
5405	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
5406	  (tag area 01CM).}
5407	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on \(\Opens X\) and let
5408	  \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). The \emph{presheaf internal hom}
5409	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules whose value on an open
5410	  \(U\) is the module of morphisms of restricted modules
5411	  \[
5412	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U)
5413	      \;:=\;
5414	    \mathtt{ModuleCat.of}\;\bigl(R(U)\bigr)\;\bigl(M|_U \longrightarrow N|_U\bigr),
5415	  \]
5416	  the \(R(U)\)-module of \(R|_U\)-linear morphisms \(M|_U \to N|_U\) of presheaves of
5417	  modules on \(U\), formed via the open-immersion restriction \(U \hookrightarrow X\).
5418	  This value module is the slice specialisation
5419	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value} of the morphism module
5420	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}.
5421	
5422	  Explicitly, \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules assembled from
5423	  three pieces. \emph{(a) Value modules.} On each open \(U\) the value is the
5424	  \(R(U)\)-module \((M|_U \to N|_U)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} /
5425	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}. \emph{(b) Restriction maps.} For an
5426	  inclusion \(V \subseteq U\) the restriction map
5427	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \to \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V)\) is the further-restriction map
5428	  \cref{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}, \(\varphi \mapsto \varphi|_V\), which
5429	  is additive and semilinear over the ring restriction \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\)
5430	  induced by \(g : V \to U\) --- the compatibility datum that the presheaf-of-modules
5431	  assembly (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.mk}\), via \(\mathtt{ofPresheaf}\)) consumes.
5432	  \emph{(c) Functoriality.} Restriction along the identity \(U \to U\) is the identity
5433	  map, and restriction along a composite \(V \xrightarrow{g} U \xrightarrow{h} W\) of
5434	  inclusions \(V \subseteq U \subseteq W\) (the morphism \(g : V \to U\) followed by
5435	  \(h : U \to W\) in \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\)) is the composite of the two
5436	  restriction maps \(\mathcal{H}om(M,N)(W) \to \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(U) \to
5437	  \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(V)\); both identity and composition are inherited from the
5438	  functoriality of further restriction, since \(\mathtt{Over.map}\) is a functor. These three pieces
5439	  make \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) a genuine, covariant presheaf of \(R\)-modules; this
5440	  assembled presheaf is the construction target \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.internalHom}\).
5441	
5442	  The slice formula is forced by contravariance: the pointwise rule
5443	  \(U \mapsto \Hom_{R(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) varies \emph{against} the restriction maps of
5444	  \(M\) and so is not a covariant presheaf, whereas the module of morphisms of
5445	  \emph{restricted} objects \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricts covariantly. Thus, unlike the
5446	  tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} --- which is covariant in the
5447	  restriction maps and sheafifies directly --- the internal hom must be built through
5448	  the slice presentation, and there is no presheaf-level dual to sheafify naively.
5449	\end{definition}
5450	
5451	\begin{lemma}
5452	
5453	\leanok
5454	[Restriction map of the presheaf internal hom]
5455	  \label{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
5456	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.restrictionMap}
5457	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value, def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}
5458	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
5459	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
5460	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3508).
5461	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
5462	  % "Consider the rule
5463	  % $$
5464	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
5465	  % $$
5466	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
5467	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
5468	  % abelian groups."
5469	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
5470	  (tag area 01CM): the rule \(U \mapsto \Hom(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)\), with its
5471	  further-restriction maps, is a (pre)sheaf of abelian groups.}
5472	  Let \(g : V \to U\) be a morphism of \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\) (an inclusion
5473	  \(V \subseteq U\) of opens), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). There
5474	  is a restriction map
5475	  \[
5476	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \longrightarrow \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V),
5477	    \qquad \varphi \longmapsto \varphi|_V,
5478	  \]
5479	  the \(\mathtt{restrictionMap}\), sending a morphism of restricted modules
5480	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to its further restriction \(M|_V \to N|_V\). Concretely, the
5481	  functor \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g : \mathcal{C}/V \to \mathcal{C}/U\) realises
5482	  ``restrict from \(U\) to \(V\)'', and \(\varphi|_V\) is \(\varphi\) pulled back along
5483	  it --- the further restriction of the same morphism of presheaves of modules. This
5484	  map is additive, and it is \emph{semilinear} over the ring restriction
5485	  \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\): for \(f \in R(U)\),
5486	  \[
5487	    (f \cdot \varphi)\big|_V \;=\; R(g)(f) \cdot \bigl(\varphi|_V\bigr).
5488	  \]
5489	  This semilinearity is the compatibility datum that the presheaf assembly of
5490	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} (through \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.ofPresheaf}\) /
5491	  \(\mathtt{mk}\)) requires.
5492	\end{lemma}
5493	
5494	\begin{proof}
5495	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
5496	  \leanok
5497	  Further restriction along \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g\) is functorial, so it carries a
5498	  morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to a morphism \(M|_V \to N|_V\) and respects identities and
5499	  composites; in particular the induced map on morphism groups is additive. For the
5500	  semilinearity, recall that the global-scalar action of
5501	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} acts on \(\varphi\) by post-composition with
5502	  the multiplication-by-\(f\) endomorphism \(m_f^{N}\); restricting that endomorphism
5503	  from \(U\) to \(V\) is multiplication by \(f|_V = R(g)(f)\) on \(N|_V\). Since further
5504	  restriction commutes with composition of morphisms, restricting \(f \cdot \varphi =
5505	  m_f^{N} \circ \varphi\) yields \(m_{R(g)(f)}^{N|_V} \circ (\varphi|_V) = R(g)(f) \cdot
5506	  (\varphi|_V)\), which is the asserted compatibility.
5507	\end{proof}
5508	
5509	\begin{definition}
5510	
5511	\leanok
5512	[Presheaf dual against the structure presheaf]
5513	  \label{def:presheaf_dual}
5514	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dual}
5515	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
5516	  With \(R\) and \(M\) as in \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}, the
5517	  \emph{presheaf dual} of \(M\) is the internal hom into the unit (structure)
5518	  presheaf,
5519	  \[
5520	    M^\vee \;:=\; \mathcal{H}om(M, R),
5521	  \]
5522	  i.e.\ \(M^\vee(U) = \mathtt{ModuleCat.of}\,(R(U))\,(M|_U \to R|_U)\), the
5523	  \(R(U)\)-module of \(R|_U\)-linear functionals on \(M|_U\). This is the
5524	  presheaf-of-modules analogue of the linear dual
5525	  \(\mathtt{Module.Dual}\,R\,M = (M \to R)\) that serves as the \(\otimes\)-inverse of
5526	  an invertible module over a fixed ring; here the fixed-ring dual is taken
5527	  open-by-open and assembled by the slice presentation of
5528	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}.
5529	\end{definition}
5530	
5531	\begin{lemma}
5532	
5533	\leanok
5534	[Evaluation/contraction of the internal hom]
5535	  \label{lem:internal_hom_eval}
5536	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.internalHomEval}
5537	  % NOTE (iter-224): \texttt{PresheafOfModules.internalHomEval} is CLOSED axiom-clean
5538	  % (\(\{\texttt{propext}, \texttt{Classical.choice}, \texttt{Quot.sound}\}\)). The full
5539	  % morphism assembles the per-open contraction \texttt{internalHomEvalApp}
5540	  % (the \(R(U)\)-bilinear \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\),
5541	  % \(s \otimes \varphi \mapsto \varphi(s)\)) sectionwise; naturality follows from the
5542	  % evaluate/restrict-commutation argument in the proof body. The iter-222/223 ``whnf
5543	  % heartbeat-bomb'' obstacle was a stale Mathlib-version artifact and is gone.
5544	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
5545	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
5546	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
5547	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3517--L3524).
5548	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
5549	  % "There is a canonical ``evaluation'' morphism
5550	  % $$
5551	  % \mathcal{F}
5552	  % \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
5553	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
5554	  % \longrightarrow
5555	  % \mathcal{G}.
5556	  % $$"
5557	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
5558	  (tag area 01CM).}
5559	  With \(R\) and \(M\) as above, there is a canonical \emph{evaluation} (contraction)
5560	  morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules
5561	  \[
5562	    \mathrm{ev}_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_R M^\vee \;\longrightarrow\; R,
5563	    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
5564	  \]
5565	  the counit of the tensor--hom adjunction
5566	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M \otimes_R -, R) \cong \mathcal{H}om(-, M^\vee)\) specialised at
5567	  \(R\). On each open \(U\) it is the open-by-open contraction
5568	  \((M|_U) \otimes_{R|_U} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R|_U\), \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto
5569	  \phi(s)\), and these are compatible with restriction, so they assemble to a morphism
5570	  of presheaves of modules. The tensor product is the presheaf tensor underlying
5571	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
5572	\end{lemma}
5573	
5574	\begin{proof}
5575	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
5576	  \leanok
5577	  On a fixed open \(U\), the value \(M^\vee(U) = (M|_U \to R|_U)\) is the module of
5578	  \(R|_U\)-linear functionals on \(M|_U\), and the evaluation
5579	  \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto \phi(s)\) is the standard contraction
5580	  \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\) bilinear over \(R(U)\), hence a
5581	  well-defined \(R(U)\)-linear map out of the tensor product. For \(V \subseteq U\)
5582	  the square relating the \(U\)- and \(V\)-contractions commutes because evaluating
5583	  then restricting equals restricting both arguments then evaluating
5584	  (\(\phi(s)|_V = (\phi|_V)(s|_V)\)); hence the open-by-open contractions are natural
5585	  in the restriction maps and define a morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules.
5586	  Concretely, the morphism is defined by specifying, on each open \(U\), the per-open
5587	  contraction \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\), \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto \phi(s)\).
5588	  The single remaining datum is the naturality square, which reduces to the
5589	  evaluate/restrict-commutation identity \(\phi(s)|_V = (\phi|_V)(s|_V)\) above: the
5590	  restriction \(\phi|_V\) of a functional inside the dual \(M^\vee\) is its restriction
5591	  to the open \(V\), and restriction is functorial, so evaluating the restricted
5592	  functional on the restricted section agrees with restricting the value of the
5593	  evaluation. This is the same restriction-functoriality argument already used to make
5594	  the internal-hom restriction maps functorial in
5595	  \cref{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}. This is the counit of the tensor--hom
5596	  adjunction of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} specialised to the unit presheaf.
5597	\end{proof}
5598	
5599	\begin{lemma}
5600	
5601	
5602	\leanok
5603	[The internal hom is a sheaf; the sheaf-level dual]
5604	  \label{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}
5605	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual}
5606	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
5607	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
5608	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
5609	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3514).
5610	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
5611	  % "Consider the rule
5612	  % $$
5613	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
5614	  % $$
5615	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
5616	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
5617	  % abelian groups. ... Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
5618	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
5619	  (tag area 01CM).}
5620	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(R = X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\), and let
5621	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with \(N\) a sheaf of modules. Then the
5622	  presheaf internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
5623	  satisfies the sheaf condition: for an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\), a family of
5624	  morphisms \(M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) agreeing on the overlaps
5625	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) glues to a unique morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\), because \(N\) is a
5626	  sheaf and morphisms of sheaves of modules are determined and gluable section-wise.
5627	  Hence \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) descends to an object of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
5628	  Specialising to \(N = \mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,R\) gives the
5629	  \emph{sheaf-level dual}
5630	  \[
5631	    \mathtt{dual}\,M \;:=\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X)
5632	    \;\in\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X,
5633	  \]
5634	  and the evaluation \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval} descends to a morphism of sheaves of
5635	  modules \(M \otimes_X \mathtt{dual}\,M \to \mathcal{O}_X\).
5636	\end{lemma}
5637	
5638	\begin{proof}
5639	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
5640	  Fix an open \(U\) and an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\). A morphism
5641	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) is a section of \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) over \(U\); restricting it
5642	  to the \(U_i\) gives a compatible family. Conversely, given morphisms
5643	  \(\varphi_i : M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) with \(\varphi_i|_{U_i \cap U_j} =
5644	  \varphi_j|_{U_i \cap U_j}\), section-wise gluing in the sheaf \(N\) produces, for
5645	  each open \(W \subseteq U\) and section \(s \in M(W)\), a well-defined section of
5646	  \(N(W)\) by gluing the \(\varphi_i(s|_{W \cap U_i})\) (these agree on overlaps by
5647	  hypothesis, and \(N\) is a sheaf so the glued section is unique). The resulting
5648	  assignment is \(R(W)\)-linear and natural in \(W\), hence a morphism
5649	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricting to the \(\varphi_i\); uniqueness is the separatedness
5650	  of \(N\). This is exactly the sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\), so it is a
5651	  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Taking \(N = \mathcal{O}_X\) yields
5652	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,M\), and the evaluation morphism of \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval},
5653	  being a morphism of presheaves into the sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\), is a morphism of
5654	  the corresponding sheaves of modules.
5655	\end{proof}
5656	
5657	\begin{lemma}
5658	
5659	
5660	\leanok
5661	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with the linear dual (H2$'$)]
5662	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5663	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoDualEquiv}
5664	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
5665	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M\) be an
5666	  \(S\)-module. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the formation of the
5667	  linear dual: the canonical map
5668	  \[
5669	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \to_S S\bigr)
5670	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
5671	    \bigl(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M \to_R R\bigr),
5672	    \qquad \varphi \;\longmapsto\; e^{-1} \circ \varphi,
5673	  \]
5674	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence, with inverse \(\psi \mapsto e \circ \psi\). On both
5675	  sides the \(R\)-module structure is the \(S\)-module structure of \(M\) pulled back
5676	  along \(e\), so that \(r \cdot m = e(r) \cdot m\): the left-hand side is the
5677	  \(S\)-linear dual \((M \to_S S)\) regarded as an \(R\)-module by restriction, and the
5678	  right-hand side is the \(R\)-linear dual of the restricted module
5679	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M\) into the restricted ground ring. This is the dual
5680	  analogue of \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}: it is to the internal hom
5681	  / dual exactly what that lemma is to the tensor product. It is the \emph{H2$'$
5682	  ingredient} of the restriction-compatibility isomorphism of the C-bridge
5683	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} --- the ring-iso/dual commutation that, composed with
5684	  the open-immersion pushforward comparison H1, identifies the dual of a restriction
5685	  with the restriction of the dual.
5686	\end{lemma}
5687	
5688	\begin{proof}
5689	
5690	\leanok
5691	  The two assignments \(\varphi \mapsto e^{-1} \circ \varphi\) and
5692	  \(\psi \mapsto e \circ \psi\) are mutually inverse, since \(e \circ e^{-1} =
5693	  \mathrm{id}_S\) and \(e^{-1} \circ e = \mathrm{id}_R\) as ring homomorphisms, so the
5694	  two composites are the identity on functionals. Each assignment is \(R\)-linear:
5695	  additivity is immediate, and for a scalar \(r \in R\) the defining relation
5696	  \(r \cdot m = e(r) \cdot m\) of the pulled-back module structure makes each linearity
5697	  check a single rewrite --- \(e^{-1}\) (resp.\ \(e\)) intertwines the \(R\)-action on
5698	  the source with the \(R\)-action on the target precisely because \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\)
5699	  are inverse ring homomorphisms identifying \(R\) with \(S\). The forward map is
5700	  therefore an \(R\)-linear bijection, i.e.\ an \(R\)-linear equivalence; packaging the
5701	  two one-sided maps with the inverse identities gives
5702	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoDualEquiv}\).
5703	\end{proof}
5704	
5705	\begin{definition}
5706	
5707	\leanok
5708	[Precomposition equivalence on dual sections]
5709	  \label{def:presheaf_dual_precomp_equiv}
5710	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dualPrecompEquiv}
5711	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual}
5712	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings and let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\)
5713	  be an isomorphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. On a fixed open \(U\), precomposition
5714	  with \(e\) gives an \(R(U)\)-linear equivalence between the dual sections
5715	  \[
5716	    \mathtt{dualPrecompEquiv}\,e\,U \;\colon\;
5717	      \bigl(M'|_U \to R|_U\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \bigl(M|_U \to R|_U\bigr),
5718	      \qquad \varphi \;\longmapsto\; \varphi \circ e|_U,
5719	  \]
5720	  with inverse \(\psi \mapsto \psi \circ (e^{-1})|_U\). This is the section-level core of
5721	  the contravariant functoriality of the presheaf dual \(M \mapsto M^\vee\)
5722	  (\cref{def:presheaf_dual}) in isomorphisms: precomposing a functional on \(M'\) with
5723	  the comparison isomorphism produces a functional on \(M\), \(R(U)\)-linearly and
5724	  invertibly.
5725	\end{definition}
5726	
5727	\begin{definition}
5728	
5729	\leanok
5730	[The presheaf dual carries an iso \(M \cong M'\) to an iso \(M'^\vee \cong M^\vee\)]
5731	  \label{def:presheaf_dual_iso_of_iso}
5732	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dualIsoOfIso}
5733	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:presheaf_dual_precomp_equiv}
5734	  An isomorphism \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules induces a
5735	  canonical isomorphism of presheaf duals
5736	  \[
5737	    \mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\,e \;\colon\; M'^\vee \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M^\vee
5738	  \]
5739	  --- note the reversal of direction, the dual being contravariant --- assembled
5740	  open-by-open from the precomposition equivalences
5741	  \(\mathtt{dualPrecompEquiv}\,e\,U\) of \cref{def:presheaf_dual_precomp_equiv}.
5742	  Naturality in \(U\), i.e.\ compatibility of these sectionwise equivalences with
5743	  restriction, is the routine check that precomposition by \(e\) commutes with
5744	  restricting functionals.
5745	\end{definition}
5746	
5747	\begin{definition}
5748	
5749	
5750	\leanok
5751	[The sheaf-level dual carries an iso \(M \cong M'\) to an iso \(\mathtt{dual}\,M' \cong \mathtt{dual}\,M\)]
5752	  \label{def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso}
5753	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dualIsoOfIso}
5754	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, def:presheaf_dual_iso_of_iso}
5755	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) an isomorphism in
5756	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a canonical isomorphism of sheaf-level
5757	  duals
5758	  \[
5759	    \mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\,e \;\colon\; \mathtt{dual}\,M' \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
5760	      \mathtt{dual}\,M,
5761	  \]
5762	  obtained by sheafifying the presheaf isomorphism \(\mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\,e\) of
5763	  \cref{def:presheaf_dual_iso_of_iso} through the sheaf-level dual of
5764	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}. It is the dual (contravariant) analogue of the
5765	  functoriality of the tensor product in isomorphisms: where an isomorphism of a tensor
5766	  factor induces an isomorphism of tensor products, an isomorphism \(M \cong M'\)
5767	  induces an isomorphism of duals in the reverse direction.
5768	\end{definition}
5769	
5770	\begin{lemma}
5771	
5772	\leanok
5773	[Open-immersion slice-site sheaf equivalence (the shared slice bridge)]
5774	  \label{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
5775	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overSliceSheafEquiv}
5776	  % SOURCE: Mathlib code-location provenance (this is NOT an external literature
5777	  % citation, so it carries no verbatim mathematical SOURCE QUOTE). This lemma
5778	  % completes the documented TODO at Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean, lines
5779	  % 19--22, reproduced here for orientation:
5780	  %   "show that both functors of the equivalence overEquivalence U are continuous
5781	  %    and induce an equivalence between Sheaf ((Opens.grothendieckTopology X).over U) A
5782	  %    and Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology U) A for any category A."
5783	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subset, with associated open
5784	  immersion \(\iota : U \hookrightarrow X\). The reindexing equivalence
5785	  \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U : \mathtt{Over}\,U \simeq \mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\)
5786	  --- between the slice of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) over \(U\) and the opens of the subspace
5787	  \(\widetilde{U}\) --- upgrades to an \emph{equivalence of sheaf categories}
5788	  \[
5789	    \mathtt{Sheaf}\bigl((\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,X).\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr)\,A
5790	      \;\;\simeq\;\;
5791	    \mathtt{Sheaf}\bigl(\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,U\bigr)\,A
5792	  \]
5793	  for any target category \(A\), compatible with the module-pullback comparison
5794	  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). This completes the Mathlib TODO recorded at
5795	  \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} (lines 19--22). It is the \emph{shared root}
5796	  underlying both the restriction-compatibility isomorphism for the dual
5797	  (\cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}) and the morphism-descent / gluing engine
5798	  (\cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}): both reduce to identifying a
5799	  slice-indexed family of sections over \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\) with an
5800	  open-immersion-indexed family over \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\), and that
5801	  identification is exactly this equivalence.
5802	\end{lemma}
5803	
5804	\begin{proof}
5805	
5806	\leanok
5807	  The functor \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U\) realises \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\) as a
5808	  \emph{dense subsite} of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\): every open of the subspace
5809	  \(\widetilde{U}\) is covered by down-sets \(\{V : V \le U\}\), and these down-sets are
5810	  precisely the objects of the slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\). One therefore applies the
5811	  dense-subsite transfer \(\mathtt{Functor.IsDenseSubsite.sheafEquiv}\): a continuous,
5812	  cocontinuous, dense inclusion of sites induces an equivalence of the associated sheaf
5813	  categories. The continuity and cover-lifting hypotheses are supplied by the slice-site
5814	  instances of \(\mathtt{Sites/Over}\) (the Grothendieck topology
5815	  \(J.\mathtt{over}\,U\) and its comparison with the topology pulled back along
5816	  \(\iota\)).
5817	
5818	  The over-category coherence that is laborious for a general site --- the pseudofunctor
5819	  identities \(\mathtt{Over.mapId}\), \(\mathtt{Over.mapComp}\) and the associativity
5820	  \(\mathtt{overMapPullback\_assoc}\) --- is here \emph{automatic}: because
5821	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) is a thin poset, every hom-set is a subsingleton, so each such
5822	  square commutes by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) (and its heterogeneous form
5823	  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.helim}\) where source and target objects differ only up to a
5824	  propositional equality of opens). The sole sectionwise content is the down-set
5825	  identification
5826	  \[
5827	    \iota_{!}\bigl(\iota^{-1}(V)\bigr) \;=\; V
5828	    \qquad (V \le U),
5829	  \]
5830	  i.e.\ the image under the open immersion of the preimage of an open contained in \(U\)
5831	  is that open again. Transporting sections along this equality and invoking the
5832	  dense-subsite equivalence yields the stated sheaf equivalence, compatible with
5833	  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\) by the same thinness argument.
5834	\end{proof}
5835	
5836	\begin{lemma}
5837	
5838	\leanok
5839	[The per-section dual transport \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\)]
5840	  \label{lem:slice_dual_transport}
5841	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sliceDualTransport}
5842	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5843	  Let \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes and let
5844	  \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Write \(\alpha\) for the open-immersion
5845	  structure-presheaf natural transformation with components
5846	  \(\alpha_U = (f.\mathtt{appIso}\,U).\mathrm{inv}\), a \emph{\(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)}-level
5847	  ring isomorphism, and \(\beta = \mathtt{whiskerRight}\,\alpha\,
5848	  (\mathtt{forget}_2\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat})\) for its
5849	  \(\mathtt{RingCat}\)-level shadow, with component
5850	  \(\beta_V \colon \mathcal{O}_Y(V) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)
5851	  (\(fV := f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(V)\)). Then for every open \(V \subseteq Y\) there is an
5852	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism
5853	  \[
5854	    \bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M.\mathtt{val})\bigr)(V)
5855	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
5856	    \bigl(\mathtt{dual}\,((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,M.\mathtt{val})\bigr)(V),
5857	  \]
5858	  realised as a single \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.toModuleIso}\) that packages \emph{both} leg (A)
5859	  --- the slice-Hom base-change reindexing across \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) --- and leg (B)
5860	  --- the unit codomain ring-iso swap. The naturality of the section family in \(W\) has
5861	  \emph{two} distinct parts: the underlying base-morphism uniqueness in
5862	  \((\mathtt{Over}\,V.\mathrm{unop})^{\mathrm{op}}\) is \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) (a thin
5863	  poset has at most one inclusion between objects), but the accompanying equation of
5864	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module maps is a genuine, separate obligation: it is the
5865	  \(\varepsilon\)-naturality of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-ring iso
5866	  \(\beta_W\), which \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) does \emph{not} discharge.
5867	\end{lemma}
5868	
5869	\begin{proof}
5870	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5871	  The isomorphism is produced from an \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear equivalence between the
5872	  left-hand module --- the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module inherited from
5873	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) --- and the right-hand \(\mathtt{internalHomObjModule}\),
5874	  assembled via \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.toModuleIso}\), with both module structures
5875	  identified explicitly.
5876	
5877	  \emph{Leg (A): the slice-Hom reindexing, built categorically.} A dual section
5878	  \(\varphi\) of the left-hand value is a morphism \(\mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,M.\mathtt{val} \to
5879	  \mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,\mathbf{1}_X\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV)\). Its image is the dual
5880	  section over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\) whose component at \(W \le V\) (with image
5881	  \(fW := f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(W)\)) is the categorical image
5882	  \[
5883	    (\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W).\mathrm{map}\,
5884	      \bigl(\varphi_{(\mathtt{Over.mk}\,(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(W \le V)))}\bigr),
5885	  \]
5886	  i.e.\ the component of \(\varphi\) at the \(f\)-image of \(W\), reindexed across
5887	  \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) by restriction of scalars along \(\beta_W\), and then
5888	  post-composed with the leg-(B) codomain swap below.
5889	
5890	  \emph{Leg (B): the unit codomain ring-iso swap, via the lax-monoidal unit \(\varepsilon\).}
5891	  The codomain of the reindexed component is the restriction of scalars of the unit section,
5892	  which must be carried to the unit section over \(Y\). This is exactly the inverse of the
5893	  lax-monoidal unit comparison of the restriction-of-scalars functor,
5894	  \[
5895	    \mathtt{codomainMap} \;:=\;
5896	      \mathtt{inv}\bigl(\varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,g)\bigr),
5897	      \qquad g := (f.\mathtt{appIso}\,W).\mathrm{inv}.\mathrm{hom},
5898	  \]
5899	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dualUnitRingSwap}
5900	  where \(g\) is taken at the \emph{\(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)} level. The unit \(\varepsilon\)
5901	  is invertible because \(g\) is a ring isomorphism
5902	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_ε_restrictScalars_appIso},
5903	  so \(\varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,g)\) is an isomorphism. The presheaf-level
5904	  identification underlying the unit handling is the dual-of-unit isomorphism for the unit
5905	  section\lean{PresheafOfModules.dualUnitIsoGen}.
5906	
5907	  \emph{Inverse.} The inverse reindexing depends on a set-theoretic down-set fact: because
5908	  \(f\) is an open immersion, \(fV \subseteq \mathrm{range}\,f\), so every open
5909	  \(W'' \le fV\) already lies in the image of \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\), namely
5910	  \(W'' = f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(f^{-1}W'')\). This is the open-image down-set coverage of an
5911	  \(\mathtt{IsOpenImmersion}\): an open contained in the (open) image of the immersion is the
5912	  functor-image of its own preimage. The project records it as the down-set identity
5913	  \(\iota_!(\iota^{-1}V) = V\) for \(V \le W\) (the helper
5914	  \(\mathtt{image\_preimage\_of\_le}\)\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.image_preimage_of_le}),
5915	  and it is exactly the lemma the inverse reindexing relies on to define the components on the
5916	  whole down-set of \(fV\).
5917	
5918	  With this in hand the inverse \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Hom}\) mirrors the forward
5919	  component formula, with two changes: it uses \((f.\mathtt{appIso}\,W'').\mathrm{hom}\) in
5920	  place of its inverse, and its \(\varepsilon\)-codomain swap is
5921	  \(\mathtt{dualUnitRingSwapHom} = \mathtt{inv}\bigl(\varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,
5922	  (f.\mathtt{appIso}\,W'').\mathrm{hom})\bigr)\)\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dualUnitRingSwapHom}
5923	  --- that is, the \emph{inverse} of \(\varepsilon\) in the \(\mathrm{hom}\)-direction (where
5924	  \(\varepsilon\) is invertible by
5925	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_ε_restrictScalars_appIso_hom}), \emph{not}
5926	  \(\varepsilon\) of the \(\mathrm{inv}\)-direction \(\beta\). (The reverse reindex turns an
5927	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fW'')\)-section map into an \(\mathcal{O}_Y(W'')\)-map, so it must restrict
5928	  along the \(\mathrm{hom}\)-direction ring iso, whose codomain swap is therefore \(\mathtt{inv}\,
5929	  \varepsilon\) of \emph{that} functor.) The whole family is reindexed by the inverse of the
5930	  down-set bijection \(W'' \leftrightarrow f^{-1}W''\) above. The round-trip identities \(\mathtt{left\_inv}\)
5931	  and \(\mathtt{right\_inv}\) then close component-wise by \(\mathtt{Iso.inv\_hom\_id}\) and
5932	  \(\mathtt{Iso.hom\_inv\_id}\) of \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\) (the forward/inverse \(\beta\)-swaps
5933	  cancel) together with the cancellation of the down-set bijection
5934	  \(\iota_!(\iota^{-1}V) = V\) against its inverse.
5935

1877	\label{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}
1878	
1879	This section supplies the one genuinely Mathlib-absent ingredient on which the
1880	\emph{unconditional} associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} rests: the commutation
1881	of the stalk functor with the relative-ring tensor product of presheaves of modules
1882	(``d.2''). Together with the already-built \(R_x\)-linear stalk comparison
1883	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, ``d.1'') it closes the single open left-whiskering
1884	obligation for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering object --- with no flatness and no
1885	local-triviality hypothesis --- which is exactly what makes the sheafification
1886	transport of the presheaf associator work for all modules. The construction is to be
1887	formalized in the dedicated Lean file
1888	\(\mathtt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean}\).
1889	
1890	\begin{lemma}
1891	
1892	\leanok
1893	[Stalk of a tensor product (d.2): varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation]
1894	  \label{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}
1895	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorIso}
1896	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1897	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Tensor product),
1898	  % Lemma lemma-stalk-tensor-product (stalk of a tensor product of modules is the
1899	  % tensor product of the stalks over the stalk ring)
1900	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2332-L2344).
1901	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1902	  % "\begin{lemma}
1903	  % \label{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}
1904	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1905	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
1906	  % Let $x \in X$. There is a canonical isomorphism
1907	  % of $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$-modules
1908	  % $$
1909	  % (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})_x
1910	  % =
1911	  % \mathcal{F}_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}} \mathcal{G}_x
1912	  % $$
1913	  % functorial in $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$.
1914	  % \end{lemma}"
1915	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1916	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}.}
1917	  Let \(R : (\mathtt{Opens}\,X)^{op} \to \mathtt{CommRingCat}\) be the structure
1918	  presheaf of a scheme \(X\), and let \(A, B\) be presheaves of
1919	  \(R\)-modules (objects of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ
1920	  \mathtt{forget_2}\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat})\)). For each point
1921	  \(x \in X\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \((R \circ
1922	  \mathtt{forget_2}).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-modules
1923	  \[
1924	    (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1925	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1926	    A.\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\otimes_{R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x}\; B.\mathtt{stalk}\,x,
1927	  \]
1928	  natural in \(A\) and \(B\), where \(\otimes^{p}\) is the presheaf-of-modules tensor
1929	  product \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\).
1930	\end{lemma}
1931	
1932	\begin{proof}
1933	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1934	  \leanok
1935	  The stalk at \(x\) is the filtered colimit over the neighbourhoods \(U \ni x\) of
1936	  the sections functor, and sectionwise the tensor product is computed pointwise:
1937	  \((A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\)
1938	  (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj\_obj}\)). The neighbourhood system
1939	  \(\{U \ni x\}\) is cofiltered under inclusion, so the colimit is filtered. The
1940	  isomorphism is assembled in five named stages. Stages (i)--(ii) construct the
1941	  forward additive comparison map and its germ characterisation; stages (iii)--(v)
1942	  upgrade it to an \(R_x\)-linear map, construct the reverse map, and check mutual
1943	  inversion.
1944	
1945	  \emph{(i) Per-neighbourhood balanced bilinear map and its section-level descent}
1946	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorBilin}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}\)). For a fixed
1947	  neighbourhood \(U \ni x\) the assignment
1948	  \[
1949	    (a, b) \;\longmapsto\; \mathrm{germ}_x\,a \,\otimes\, \mathrm{germ}_x\,b,
1950	    \qquad a \in A(U),\ b \in B(U),
1951	  \]
1952	  is \(R(U)\)-balanced bilinear into \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\): it is additive in each
1953	  variable, and for \(r \in R(U)\) the two ways of moving \(r\) across the tensor agree
1954	  after passing to germs, since \(\mathrm{germ}_x(r \cdot a) = (\mathrm{germ}_x r)
1955	  \cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a\) and the stalk tensor is balanced over
1956	  \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) (the germ--scalar compatibility of
1957	  \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}). By the universal property of the relative tensor
1958	  product it descends to an additive map on the section module
1959	  \[
1960	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U \;\colon\;
1961	      (A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)
1962	        \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1963	    \qquad a \otimes b \longmapsto \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b.
1964	  \]
1965	
1966	  \emph{(ii) Colimit descent to the forward comparison map}
1967	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). The
1968	  maps \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U\) are compatible with the restriction maps of the
1969	  presheaf \(A \otimes^{p} B\) as \(U\) shrinks toward \(x\) (a smaller neighbourhood
1970	  computes the same germs), so they form a cocone over the filtered diagram
1971	  \(\{(A \otimes^{p} B)(U)\}_{U \ni x}\). The induced map out of the colimit is the
1972	  forward additive comparison map
1973	  \[
1974	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc} \;\colon\;
1975	      (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1976	  \]
1977	  characterised on germs of simple tensors by
1978	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\bigr) =
1979	  \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b\)
1980	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). This is the forward half of the asserted
1981	  isomorphism (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_desc_forward}).
1982	
1983	  \emph{(iii) \(R_x\)-linearity packaging} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\)). The
1984	  additive map of (ii) is upgraded to an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear map: on a germ
1985	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(U)\) one checks
1986	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \xi\bigr) =
1987	  (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}(\xi)\) on germs of simple tensors
1988	  and extends by additivity. The one subtlety is a \emph{carrier-duality obstacle}:
1989	  the section-level tensor \(A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\) is a module over the
1990	  \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier \((R \circ \mathtt{forget_2})(U)\), whereas the natural
1991	  scalar \(r : R(U)\) lives in the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) carrier \(R(U)\); the two
1992	  carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, so the
1993	  scalar-pulling lemma \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\) applies only after
1994	  inserting a canonical \(\mathtt{RingEquiv}\) bridge between the
1995	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carriers. Once that bridge is
1996	  in place the linearity computation mirrors the germ-representative pattern used
1997	  for the d.1 stalk map \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) (\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}).
1998	
1999	  \emph{(iv) The reverse map.} The map
2000	  \[
2001	    A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \;\longrightarrow\; (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
2002	  \]
2003	  is built by the universal property of the tensor product over \(R_x\) from the
2004	  \(R_x\)-bilinear map of the two stalks
2005	  \[
2006	    (\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b) \;\longmapsto\;
2007	      \mathrm{germ}_x\bigl(a|_{U \cap V} \otimes b|_{U \cap V}\bigr),
2008	  \]
2009	  where \(a \in A(U)\) and \(b \in B(V)\) are chosen representatives; this is a
2010	  \emph{nested} colimit descent, descending first the \(A\)-variable out of the
2011	  filtered colimit \(\{A(U)\}_{U \ni x}\) and then the \(B\)-variable out of
2012	  \(\{B(V)\}_{V \ni x}\), passing to the common refinement \(U \cap V\) to form the
2013	  simple tensor and its germ. Well-definedness on germs (independence of the chosen
2014	  representatives and of the neighbourhoods \(U, V\)) is exactly filteredness of the
2015	  neighbourhood system.
2016	
2017	  It remains to check that the reverse map is \(R_x\)-bilinear, and in particular that
2018	  its underlying bilinear map is \emph{balanced} over \(R_x\), i.e. that pulling a scalar
2019	  out of the left variable agrees with pulling it out of the right. The key point is to
2020	  establish this balancing \emph{at the stalk level}, with every scalar living in the
2021	  stalk ring \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) throughout. Concretely, for a germ
2022	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(W)\) one verifies the identity
2023	  \[
2024	    \mathtt{revBihom}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b\bigr)
2025	      \;=\; (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{revBihom}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b\bigr),
2026	  \]
2027	  and symmetrically on the right variable, where the scalar \(\mathrm{germ}_x r \in R_x\)
2028	  never leaves the stalk ring. This holds because of the germ--scalar compatibility
2029	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x(r \cdot s) = (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot(\mathrm{germ}_x s)\) of the germ
2030	  map \(R(W) \to R_x\) (the Lean lemma \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\)) — the same germ--scalar
2031	  compatibility already used in stages (i) and (iii). Multiplying a representative
2032	  \(r \cdot a\) and then taking germs produces \((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a\),
2033	  so the action of \(r\) is transparently the \(R_x\)-action on the stalk and may be
2034	  moved freely across the tensor of stalks, where balancedness over \(R_x\) is built into
2035	  \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\). Because the whole computation takes place over the single
2036	  ring \(R_x\), no carrier identification intervenes and the balancing follows directly.
2037	
2038	  One must \emph{not} instead reduce this balancing to a section-level identity. If the
2039	  scalar were pushed through the presheaf restriction to a common neighbourhood \(W\) and
2040	  the balancing read off inside the section module \(A(W) \otimes_{R(W)} B(W)\), then the
2041	  scalar action appearing there is the one produced by the presheaf-of-modules structure
2042	  of \(A\) via restriction of scalars: it lives over the \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier
2043	  \((R \circ \mathtt{forget_2})(W)\), \emph{not} over the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) carrier
2044	  \(R(W)\) that annotates the section tensor. This is precisely the
2045	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-vs-\(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier-duality obstacle already met in
2046	  stage (iii), here compounded by the extra restriction-of-scalars wrapper, so the naive
2047	  ``move the scalar across the tensor'' step (\(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\)) does
2048	  not apply at the section level without first inserting the carrier bridge. Working at the
2049	  stalk level as above sidesteps this wrapper entirely.
2050	
2051	  Where a \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-vs-\(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier identification is
2052	  genuinely unavoidable, it is the \emph{same} canonical bridge already invoked for the
2053	  stage-(iii) \(R_x\)-linearity packaging (the \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU\_smul}\)/%
2054	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\) step, \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_linear_map}): the two
2055	  carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, and the same
2056	  \(\mathtt{RingEquiv}\) bridge resolves the obstruction here. Thus the stage-(iv)
2057	  balancing reuses the technique already resolved in stage (iii) rather than re-deriving
2058	  it.
2059	
2060	  \emph{(v) Mutual inversion on germ generators} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\)). The two
2061	  composites are checked to be identities on generators. The composite
2062	  \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \to (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \to A_x
2063	  \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) is the identity on a simple tensor \(\mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes
2064	  \mathrm{germ}_x b\) by the germ characterisation \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)
2065	  of (ii), and extends to all of \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) by
2066	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\). The opposite composite
2067	  \((A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \to A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \to (A \otimes^{p}
2068	  B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) is the identity on a germ \(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\) of
2069	  a simple tensor by the same characterisation; since germs generate the stalk and the
2070	  germ maps are jointly epimorphic (\(\mathtt{stalk\_hom\_ext}\)), together with
2071	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\) applied to each section, the composite is the
2072	  identity. Bundling the \(R_x\)-linear forward map of (iii) with the reverse map of
2073	  (iv) and these two inversion identities yields the asserted isomorphism
2074	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\).
2075	
2076	  Naturality in \(A, B\) is inherited from naturality of the sectionwise tensor and of
2077	  the germ maps. This is the presheaf-of-modules, varying-ring analogue of the Stacks
2078	  lemma above; the only subtlety beyond the ringed-space statement is that the ground
2079	  ring varies with \(U\), handled by the ``tensor of filtered colimits over the colimit
2080	  ring'' identification together with the carrier-duality bridge of (iii). Conceptually
2081	  the tensor product of modules commutes with filtered colimits in each variable and
2082	  the ground ring \(R(U)\) itself colimits to the stalk ring \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\),
2083	  which is the reason the forward map (ii) and the reverse map (iv) are mutually
2084	  inverse.
2085	\end{proof}

input.command:
cd /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge; python3 - <<'PY'
import re
p='blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex'
s=open(p).read()

# Operate only on the three new subsections. Identify regions by their subsection labels.
markers=[
 r'\subsection{Stalk--tensor comparison helper declarations}',
 r'\subsection{Internal-hom and restrict-scalars helper declarations}',
]
# Group B region: from its subsection to the following \section{
b_start=s.index(markers[0])
b_end=s.index(r'\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility', b_start)
# Group A+C region: from its subsection to EOF
a_start=s.index(markers[1])
a_end=len(s)

def transform(region):
    # For each block: a \lean{...} line inside an env, followed (after \end{env}) by
    # \begin{proof}\n  \uses{...}. Insert "  \uses{...}" right after the \lean line.
    pat=re.compile(
        r'(\\lean\{[^}]*\}\n)'          # 1: lean line
        r'(.*?'                          # 2: statement body
        r'\\end\{(?:definition|lemma)\}\n'
        r'\\begin\{proof\}\n'
        r'\s*\\uses\{([^}]*)\})',        # 3: uses content
        re.DOTALL)
    def repl(m):
        return m.group(1)+'  \\uses{'+m.group(3)+'}\n'+m.group(2)
    return pat.sub(repl,region)

regionB=s[b_start:b_end]
regionA=s[a_start:a_end]
newB=transform(regionB)
newA=transform(regionA)
# count insertions
print('B uses inserted:',newB.count('\\uses{')-regionB.count('\\uses{'))
print('A uses inserted:',newA.count('\\uses{')-regionA.count('\\uses{'))
s2=s[:b_start]+newB+s[b_end:a_start]+newA+s[a_end:]
open(p,'w').write(s2)
print('done')
PY
